citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The primary enclosures are not being sufficiently cleaned. Three primary enclosures housing 1 adult Dachshund in each
enclosure were observed with excessive piles of excreta throughout the outdoor portion of the enclosures. Two of the
enclosures were observed with approximately 15 piles of excreta and the third enclosure was observed with
approximately 25 piles of excreta. The excreta observed in these enclosures varied from fresh with a moist appearance to
very dry with a whitish appearance. When inquiring with the facility representative how often excreta is removed from
enclosures, he stated it was removed weekly from the outdoor section of the enclosures and daily in the indoor portion of
the enclosures.
Failure to remove excreta from primary enclosures daily can result in an accumulation of excreta which can lead to
increased disease hazards, discomfort for the animals, increased odors, and/or attract unwanted pests.
Excreta and other waste must be removed from primary enclosures daily to prevent an excessive accumulation of feces,
to prevent soiling of the dogs contained in the primary enclosures, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and
odors.
Correct by 26 May 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
n

narrative:
3.11(a) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The primary enclosures are not being sufficiently cleaned. Three primary enclosures housing 1 adult Dachshund in each 
enclosure were observed with excessive piles of excreta throughout the outdoor portion of the enclosures. Two of the 
enclosures were observed with approximately 15 piles of excreta and the third enclosure was observed with

very dry with a whitish appearance. When inquiring with the facility representative how often excreta is removed from 
enclosures, he stated it was removed weekly from the outdoor section of the enclosures and daily in the indoor portion of 
the enclosures.  
 
Failure to remove excreta from primary enclosures daily can result in an accumulation of excreta which can lead to 
increased disease hazards, discomfort for the animals, increased odors, and/or attract unwanted pests. 
 
Excreta and other waste must be removed from primary enclosures daily to prevent an excessive accumulation of feces, 
to prevent soiling of the dogs contained in the primary enclosures, and to reduce disease hazards, insects, pests, and 
odors.  
Correct by 26 May 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
